chunk_summary: Fortified foods and home fortification options, such as micronutrient powders and small-quantity lipid-based nutrient supplements, can address nutrient gaps in children 6–24 months when dietary diversity is insufficient, as per WHO 2023 guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of fortified foods and home fortification in addressing nutrient gaps during complementary feeding.
Learn the appropriate use of multiple micronutrient powders (MNP) and small-quantity lipid-based nutrient supplements (SQ-LNS).
Identify contexts where fortified foods or nutrient supplements are necessary for children aged 6–24 months.
Recognize the importance of monitoring clinical response to fortified food interventions.
key_facts: WHO 2023 guidelines recommend fortified foods or nutrient supplements for children 6–23 months in contexts where dietary diversity is insufficient.
Multiple micronutrient powders (MNP) are mixed into semi-solid food to improve micronutrient intake but should not be added to very hot food.
Small-quantity lipid-based nutrient supplements (SQ-LNS) provide essential fatty acids and micronutrients in high-risk settings.
Fortified products should not replace meals or be marketed as routine snacks for healthy toddlers.
Clinical monitoring of appetite, stool tolerance, dietary displacement, and growth is recommended over 4–8 weeks after initiating fortified food use.
reference_quote: "The 2023 WHO complementary feeding guideline notes that in some contexts, where requirements cannot be met with unfortified foods alone, children 6–23 months may benefit from nutrient supplements or fortified food products." (Reference 2)
document_key_topics: Clinical monitoring of fortified food interventions
